mission.title: Install and Configure Holmesgpt on Kubernetes
mission.description: HolmesGPT is an open-source AI agent designed for investigating production incidents and identifying root causes. As a CNCF Sandbox project, it integrates with various observability tools to enhance incident response capabilities.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the HolmesGPT Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration using the following command:
```bash
helm repo add holmes https://holmesgpt.dev/helm
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install HolmesGPT
steps.description:
Install HolmesGPT in your Kubernetes cluster with the specified version and create a namespace:
```bash
helm install holmes holmes/holmes --version 0.20.0 --namespace holmes --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the HolmesGPT pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace holmes
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Update the resource limits for the HolmesGPT deployment by editing the values file or using the following command:
```bash
helm upgrade holmes holmes/holmes --namespace holmes --set resources.requests.cpu=100m --set resources.requests.memory=2048Mi --set resources.limits.memory=2048Mi
```

steps.title: Set up monitoring
steps.description:
Enable monitoring by configuring the Prometheus toolset in HolmesGPT:
```bash
helm upgrade holmes holmes/holmes --namespace holmes --set toolsets.prometheus/metrics.enabled=true
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of HolmesGPT will show the pods running in the 'holmes' namespace. You can verify the configuration by checking the resource limits and ensuring that the Prometheus toolset is enabled.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add holmes https://holmesgpt.dev/helm
helm repo update
helm upgrade holmes holmes/holmes --namespace holmes --set resources.requests.cpu=100m --set resources.requests.memory=2048Mi --set resources.limits.memory=2048Mi
helm upgrade holmes holmes/holmes --namespace holmes --set toolsets.prometheus/metrics.enabled=true

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the HolmesGPT release from your Kubernetes cluster using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall holmes --namespace holmes
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep holmes | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace holmes
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the namespace used for the installation and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace holmes
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and resources:
```bash
helm get values holmes --namespace holmes > holmes-backup-values.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the HolmesGPT installation to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade holmes holmes/holmes --version 0.21.0 --namespace holmes
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the HolmesGPT pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace holmes
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see pods in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace holmes
``` 
Look for any specific error messages that indicate what might be wrong.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the service is not reachable, verify the service status and endpoints:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace holmes
kubectl describe svc <service-name> --namespace holmes
``` 
Ensure that the service is correctly configured and that the pods are healthy.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are not starting due to resource constraints, check the resource requests and limits:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace holmes
``` 
Adjust the resource limits in your Helm configuration if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Configuration changes not applied
troubleshooting.description:
If changes to the configuration are not reflected, ensure you are using the correct upgrade command:
```bash
helm upgrade holmes holmes/holmes --namespace holmes --set <your-configuration>
``` 
Double-check the values you are setting to ensure they are correct.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, and that Helm and kubectl are installed on your local machine.
